content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).

8	/-!
9	# Auslander–Buchsbaum formula (A.4.b)
10	
11	This file is the **A.4.b** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
12	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the
13	Auslander–Buchsbaum formula and the corollary "regular local ring
14	⟹ Cohen–Macaulay" that the sibling sub-build **A.4.a** (codim-1 extension of a
15	rational map across a codim-2 closed point on a regular projective surface)
16	consumes.
17	
18	Per STRATEGY.md L30 this row is gated downstream on A.4.a but is independently
19	startable on the Mathlib side: the algebra here is decoupled from the Albanese
20	geometry and lives entirely under `RingTheory.*`. Mathlib at the project's
21	pinned commit (`b80f227`) exposes `IsRegularLocalRing` and the categorical
22	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` on an abelian category (specialised to
23	`ModuleCat R`), as well as the regular-sequence definition
24	`RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular`. The depth function `Module.depth`, the
25	Auslander–Buchsbaum formula, and the Cohen–Macaulay predicate are *not* in
26	Mathlib at the pinned commit — they are the new content scaffolded here.
27	
28	## Status (iter-178 update — projectiveDimension closed)
29	
30	This file was originally landed iter-175 (Lane F file-skeleton) with each
31	blueprint-pinned declaration carrying the *intended* substantive type
32	signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
33	`blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum.tex`). The bodies were
34	scheduled for iter-176+ work; the chapter is independent of the rest of
35	Route A's geometric infrastructure, making it a good parallel-work
36	candidate.
37	
38	iter-178 closed `Module.projectiveDimension` kernel-clean as a one-liner
39	re-export of the categorical `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension`. The
40	remaining five typed `sorry` bodies (`depth`, `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`,
41	`depth_of_short_exact`, `auslander_buchsbaum_formula`,
42	`CohenMacaulay.of_regular`) are substantive multi-iter content and stay
43	gated on dedicated body lanes.
44	
45	The 7 pinned declarations are:
46	
47	1. `RingTheory.Module.depth` (noncomputable def, ~6 LOC) — the `I`-depth of a
48	   finite `R`-module as the supremum of lengths of `M`-regular sequences in
49	   `I`, valued in `ℕ∞`.
50	2. `RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — for
51	   `(R, 𝔪)` Noetherian local and `M ≠ 0` finite, `depth(M)` equals the
52	   smallest `i` with `Ext^i_R(κ, M) ≠ 0`. Encoded via the depth-bound iff
53	   `Ext` vanishes below.
54	3. `Module.projectiveDimension` (noncomputable def, ~3 LOC) — an
55	   `R`-module-side wrapper for the categorical
56	   `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R M)`. The categorical
57	   version exists in Mathlib `b80f227`; this is the re-export pinned by the
58	   blueprint.
59	4. `RingTheory.depth_of_short_exact` (theorem, ~12 LOC) — the
60	   depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequalities (Stacks 00LE).
61	5. `RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the formula
62	   `pd_R(M) + depth(M) = depth(R)` for a nonzero finite `R`-module of finite
63	   projective dimension over a Noetherian local ring.
64	6. `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay` (class, ~3 LOC) — `IsCohenMacaulayLocalRing R`
65	   encoded as `depth(R) = krullDim R`.
66	7. `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular` (theorem, ~6 LOC) — a regular local
67	   ring is Cohen–Macaulay (the consumer-facing input for A.4.a).
68	
69	## Note on type expressivity
70	
71	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof", each
72	declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
73	
74	- `depth` returns `ℕ∞` and is defined by the regular-sequence supremum.
75	- `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` is encoded as the depth-bound `↔` `Ext`
76	  vanishing characterisation (the smallest non-vanishing `Ext` index = depth).
77	- `projectiveDimension` re-exports the categorical
78	  `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` on `ModuleCat.of R M`.
79	- `depth_of_short_exact` packages the three Stacks 00LE inequalities into a
80	  conjunction.
81	- `auslander_buchsbaum_formula` is the numeric equation
82	  `pd + depth = depth(R)`.
83	- `CohenMacaulay` is the equation `depth(R) = ringKrullDim R`.
84	- `CohenMacaulay.of_regular` is an `IsRegularLocalRing → IsCohenMacaulayLocalRing`
85	  implication, the consumer-facing statement.
86	
87	Unfolding any declaration exposes the named substantive content (a regular-
88	sequence supremum, an `Ext`-vanishing characterisation, the categorical
89	projective dimension, …); no `Iso.refl _` / `Classical.choice ⟨witness⟩` /
90	empty-content `proof_wanted` placeholders are used.
91	
92	## References
93	
94	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum.tex` (~560 LOC,
95	7 pins). Stacks Project tags 00LF (definition-depth), 00LP (lemma-depth-ext),
96	00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses), 090V (proposition-Auslander–Buchsbaum), 00N4
97	(definition-local-ring-CM), 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM). Matsumura,
98	*Commutative Ring Theory*, Theorem 19.1. Auslander–Buchsbaum, "Homological
99	dimension in local rings", 1957.

102	set_option autoImplicit false

104	universe u v

106	open CategoryTheory
107	
108	namespace RingTheory
109	
110	namespace Module

112	/-! ## §1. Depth of a finite module
113	
114	The `I`-depth of a finite `R`-module `M` is the supremum in
115	`{0, 1, 2, …, ∞}` of the lengths of `M`-regular sequences contained in `I`
116	(provided `IM ≠ M`; if `IM = M` we set `depth_I(M) = ∞`). Mathlib `b80f227`
117	exposes the regular-sequence predicate `RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular`
118	(`Mathlib.RingTheory.Regular.RegularSequence`) but not the resulting
119	numeric depth function — that is the gap this declaration fills.
120	
121	Blueprint reference: `def:depth` (Stacks tag 00LF). -/
122	
123	/-- The **`I`-depth** of a finite `R`-module `M`: the supremum (in `ℕ∞`) of
124	lengths of `M`-regular sequences contained in the ideal `I`.
125	
126	When `IM = M` (the "trivial-quotient" case, e.g. `M = 0` or `I = R`) the
127	supremum is taken to be `⊤` by convention. When `(R, 𝔪)` is local one usually
128	calls `depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M` simply *the depth* of `M`.
129	
130	iter-176+: the body is the supremum
131	```
132	sSup { (n : ℕ∞) | ∃ rs : List R, rs.length = n ∧ (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ I) ∧
133	                  RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular M rs }
134	```
135	folded with the `IM = M` clause. The signature is non-tautological: it
136	asserts a function `(Ideal R) → (M : Type v) → ℕ∞` matching the Stacks
137	00LF definition.
138	
139	**iter-179 Mathlib-gap check (Lane F Target 2 STRETCH)**: a pinned-commit
140	audit of `Mathlib.RingTheory.Regular.Depth` (the only file under
141	`Mathlib/RingTheory/*` containing the word "depth") confirms that Mathlib
142	`b80f227` ships only depth-zero lemmas (`IsSMulRegular.subsingleton_linearMap_iff`)
143	and *not* the numeric depth function itself. The one-liner re-export route
144	is therefore not available; the body stays a typed `sorry` until an
145	iter-180+ body lane fills the supremum-with-`IM=M` clause directly. -/
146	noncomputable def depth {R : Type u} [CommRing R] (_I : Ideal R)
147	    (_M : Type v) [AddCommGroup _M] [Module R _M] : ℕ∞ :=
148	  open Classical in
149	  if _I • (⊤ : Submodule R _M) = ⊤ then (⊤ : ℕ∞)
150	  else sSup { n : ℕ∞ | ∃ rs : List R, (rs.length : ℕ∞) = n ∧
151	    (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ _I) ∧ RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular _M rs }
152	
153	end Module
154	
155	end RingTheory
156	
157	/-! ## §2. Projective dimension
158	
159	Mathlib `b80f227` exposes the categorical
160	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension : C → WithBot ℕ∞` on an abelian category
161	with enough projectives (file `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.Projective.Dimension`).
162	For `R`-modules this specialises to `ModuleCat.of R M`. The blueprint pins the
163	`Module.projectiveDimension` name as the re-export that downstream consumers
164	can use directly on an `R`-module without first packaging it in `ModuleCat`.
165	
166	Blueprint reference: `def:projective_dimension`. -/
167	
168	namespace Module
169	
170	/-- The **projective dimension** of an `R`-module `M`, defined as the
171	categorical projective dimension of `ModuleCat.of R M`.
172	
173	This is a re-export of `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` specialised to
174	the abelian category `ModuleCat R`. The categorical definition is the
175	infimum (in `WithBot ℕ∞`) of `n : ℕ` such that all `Ext^i(M, -)` vanish for
176	`i > n`, equivalently the smallest length of a projective resolution of `M`.
177	
178	Mathlib has the categorical `projectiveDimension` and the module-specific
179	`ModuleCat.projectiveDimension_eq_of_linearEquiv`; the blueprint pins the
180	wrapper name so downstream consumers can write
181	`Module.projectiveDimension R M` rather than threading `ModuleCat.of`.
182	
183	The body is the one-line re-export
184	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R _M)`; iter-178 closed
185	this declaration kernel-clean. -/
186	noncomputable def projectiveDimension (R : Type u) [Ring R]
187	    (_M : Type u) [AddCommGroup _M] [Module R _M] : WithBot ℕ∞ :=
188	  CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R _M)
189	
190	end Module
191	
192	namespace RingTheory
193	
194	namespace Module
195	
196	/-! ## §3. Depth via Ext characterisation
197	
198	For a Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` with residue field `κ = R/𝔪` and a
199	nonzero finite `R`-module `M`, the depth of `M` equals the smallest index `i`
200	at which `Ext^i_R(κ, M)` is nonzero (Stacks tag 00LP).
201	
202	This is the lemma A.4.a's downstream consumer ultimately reads off:
203	combined with the regular-sequence definition (`depth`), the Ext
204	characterisation provides both the *lower bound* (regular sequences exhibit
205	`Ext^i = 0` for `i < length(rs)`) and the *upper bound* (failure of any
206	extension lifts a non-zero element in `Ext^{depth}(κ, M)`).
207	
208	The signature pins the equivalence via the depth-bound `↔` `Ext`-vanishing-
209	below: `n ≤ depth(M) ↔ Ext^i(κ, M) = 0 for all i < n`. This is logically
210	equivalent to "depth(M) = smallest i with Ext^i ≠ 0" and is the form most
211	convenient for inductive proofs (Stacks 00LP proof: pick `x ∈ 𝔪`
212	non-zero-divisor, use long exact `Ext^*(κ, -)` on `0 → M → M → M/xM → 0`).
213	
214	Blueprint reference: `lem:depth_via_ext` (Stacks tag 00LP). -/
215	
216	/-- **Depth via Ext characterisation.** For a Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)`
217	with residue field `κ = R/𝔪` and a nonzero finite `R`-module `M`, the
218	depth-bound `n ≤ depth(M)` is equivalent to the vanishing of `Ext^i_R(κ, M)`
219	for all `i < n`. Equivalently, `depth(M)` is the smallest `i` at which
220	`Ext^i_R(κ, M)` is nonzero.
221	
222	iter-176+: the body proceeds by induction on `n` via the long exact sequence
223	of `Ext^*(κ, -)` applied to `0 → M → M → M/xM → 0` for a non-zero-divisor
224	`x ∈ 𝔪`. The base case `n = 0` is `Hom(κ, M) ≠ 0 ↔ depth(M) = 0`, which is
225	the standard "the maximal ideal contains a zero-divisor on `M` iff
226	`𝔪 ∈ Ass(M)`" characterisation (Stacks 00LC). For the iter-175 file-skeleton
227	the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
228	theorem depth_eq_smallest_ext_index
229	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
230	    {M : Type u} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [_root_.Module.Finite R M]
231	    [Nontrivial M] (n : ℕ) :
232	    (n : ℕ∞) ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M ↔
233	      ∀ i : ℕ, i < n →
234	        ∀ e : Abelian.Ext.{u}
235	            (ModuleCat.of R (IsLocalRing.ResidueField R))
236	            (ModuleCat.of R M) i, e = 0 := by
237	  sorry
238	
239	/-! ## §4. Depth on a short exact sequence
240	
241	For a short exact sequence `0 → N' → N → N'' → 0` of nonzero finite modules
242	over a Noetherian local ring, the three modules' depths satisfy three
243	crosswise inequalities (Stacks tag 00LE), each a direct read-off of the
244	long exact `Ext^*(κ, -)` sequence and the depth-via-Ext characterisation
245	of §3.
246	
247	Blueprint reference: `lem:depth_short_exact_sequence` (Stacks tag 00LE). -/
248	
249	/-- **Depth on a short exact sequence.** Let `(R, 𝔪)` be a Noetherian local
250	ring and let
251	```
252	0 → N' → N → N'' → 0
253	```
254	be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite `R`-modules (encoded by an
255	injection `f : N' →ₗ[R] N`, a surjection `g : N →ₗ[R] N''`, and
256	`Function.Exact f g`). Then
257	1. `depth(N) ≥ min(depth(N'), depth(N''))`,
258	2. `depth(N'') ≥ min(depth(N), depth(N') - 1)`,
259	3. `depth(N') ≥ min(depth(N), depth(N'') + 1)`.
260	
261	The three inequalities are read off from the long exact `Ext^*(κ, -)`
262	sequence applied to the short exact sequence and the depth-via-Ext
263	characterisation (§3 above).
264	
265	iter-176+: the body invokes `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` on each module
266	and chases the long exact sequence. For the iter-175 file-skeleton the
267	body is a typed `sorry`. -/
268	theorem depth_of_short_exact
269	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
270	    {N' N N'' : Type u}
271	    [AddCommGroup N'] [Module R N'] [_root_.Module.Finite R N'] [Nontrivial N']
272	    [AddCommGroup N] [Module R N] [_root_.Module.Finite R N] [Nontrivial N]
273	    [AddCommGroup N''] [Module R N''] [_root_.Module.Finite R N''] [Nontrivial N'']
274	    (f : N' →ₗ[R] N) (g : N →ₗ[R] N'')
275	    (_hf : Function.Injective f) (_hg : Function.Surjective g)
276	    (_hex : Function.Exact f g) :
277	    min (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N')
278	        (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N'')
279	      ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N
280	    ∧ min (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N)
281	          (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N' - 1)
282	        ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N''
283	    ∧ min (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N)
284	          (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N'' + 1)
285	        ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) N' := by
286	  sorry
287	
288	end Module
289	
290	/-! ## §5. The Auslander–Buchsbaum formula
291	
292	For a nonzero finite module `M` of finite projective dimension over a
293	Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)`, the **Auslander–Buchsbaum formula** reads
294	```
295	  pd_R(M) + depth(M) = depth(R)
296	```
297	(Stacks tag 090V). The proof inducts on `depth(M)`: the base case
298	`depth(M) = 0` uses a minimal finite free resolution of `M` and the
299	"what is exact" criterion (Stacks 00MF) plus iterated application of the
300	depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma (§4) to bound `depth(R)` against the
301	resolution length; the inductive step picks a common non-zero-divisor
302	`x ∈ 𝔪` on both `R` and `M`, applies the snake lemma to obtain a minimal
303	finite free resolution of `M/xM` over `R/xR` of the same length, and uses
304	the inductive hypothesis on `M/xM` over `R/xR`.
305	
306	Blueprint reference: `thm:auslander_buchsbaum` (Stacks tag 090V). -/
307	
308	/-- **The Auslander–Buchsbaum formula.** Let `(R, 𝔪)` be a Noetherian local
309	ring and let `M` be a nonzero finite `R`-module of finite projective
310	dimension. Then
311	```
312	  pd_R(M) + depth_R(M) = depth(R).
313	```
314	
315	The hypothesis "finite projective dimension" is encoded by an explicit
316	upper bound `n : ℕ` on the projective dimension (so the formula compares
317	finite numeric quantities cleanly without `WithBot ℕ∞`-arithmetic
318	subtleties).
319	
320	iter-176+: the body is the induction on `depth(M)` outlined in the
321	chapter — base case via the minimal-finite-free-resolution +
322	"what is exact" + iterated `Module.depth_of_short_exact`; inductive step
323	via the snake lemma on multiplication by a common non-zero-divisor and
324	the inductive hypothesis on `M/xM` over `R/xR`. For the iter-175
325	file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
326	theorem auslander_buchsbaum_formula
327	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
328	    {M : Type u} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [_root_.Module.Finite R M]
329	    [Nontrivial M]
330	    (n : ℕ)
331	    (_hpd : _root_.Module.projectiveDimension R M = (n : WithBot ℕ∞)) :
332	    (n : ℕ∞) + Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M
333	      = Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R := by
334	  sorry
335	
336	/-! ## §6. Cohen–Macaulay local rings
337	
338	A Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` is **Cohen–Macaulay** if its depth equals
339	its Krull dimension (Stacks tag 00N4). Mathlib `b80f227` has neither the
340	predicate nor the class — this file is the upstream gap-fill.
341	
342	Blueprint reference: `def:cohen_macaulay_local` (Stacks tag 00N4). -/
343	
344	/-- A Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` is **Cohen–Macaulay** if its depth
345	equals its Krull dimension: `depth(R) = dim R`.
346	
347	Encoded as a `Prop`-valued type class so downstream consumers can write
348	`[CohenMacaulay R]` and use Cohen–Macaulay as a hypothesis. Mathlib at the
349	pinned commit (`b80f227`) does not expose any Cohen–Macaulay predicate;
350	this is the upstream gap-fill.
351	
352	iter-176+: the predicate is `Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R =
353	ringKrullDim R`. For the iter-175 file-skeleton the carrier definition is a
354	typed `sorry` at the `Prop` level — substantively, the predicate is the
355	named equality, but we package it as a `class` so use sites are uniform. -/
356	class CohenMacaulay (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R]
357	    [IsNoetherianRing R] : Prop where
358	  /-- The Cohen–Macaulay equation: `depth(R) = ringKrullDim R`. The numeric
359	  comparison is in `WithBot ℕ∞` after coercion of the `ℕ∞`-valued depth. -/
360	  depth_eq_krullDim :
361	    (Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R : WithBot ℕ∞) = ringKrullDim R
362	
363	/-! ## §7. Regular local rings are Cohen–Macaulay
364	
365	The consumer-facing input for A.4.a: every regular Noetherian local ring is
366	Cohen–Macaulay (Stacks tag 00OD). The direct proof: pick a minimal
367	generating set `x_1, …, x_d` of `𝔪` (where `d = dim R`), use that `R` is a
368	domain (Stacks 00NQ) to start an `R`-regular sequence, and induct on
369	dimension — each `R/(x_1, …, x_c)` is again regular of dimension `d - c`,
370	so `x_1, …, x_d` is an `R`-regular sequence and `depth(R) ≥ d`. The reverse
371	inequality `depth(R) ≤ dim R` is the standard depth bound (Stacks 00LK).
372	
373	Blueprint reference: `cor:regular_cohen_macaulay` (Stacks tag 00OD). -/
374	
375	namespace CohenMacaulay
376	
377	/-- **Regular local rings are Cohen–Macaulay.** Every regular Noetherian
378	local ring is Cohen–Macaulay: a minimal generating set of `𝔪` is an
379	`R`-regular sequence of length `dim R`, so `depth(R) ≥ dim R`; combined
380	with the standard upper bound `depth(R) ≤ dim R` (Stacks 00LK) this gives
381	`depth(R) = dim R`.
382	
383	This is the consumer-facing input for **A.4.a** (codim-1 extension of a
384	rational map across a codim-2 closed point on a regular projective
385	surface): the local ring `O_{S,x}` of a regular projective surface at a
386	closed point is regular of Krull dimension `2`, hence Cohen–Macaulay,
387	hence has depth `2`, which is exactly the input the local-cohomology
388	vanishing `H^i_x(O_S) = 0` for `i < 2` needs (Stacks 0AVF; see Hartshorne
389	III.7).
390	
391	iter-176+: the body is either the direct regular-sequence argument
392	outlined in the chapter, or via Auslander–Buchsbaum applied to all finite
393	`R`-modules (any finite `R`-module has `pd_R(M) ≤ d`; specialising to
394	suitable `M` gives the equation). For the iter-175 file-skeleton the body
395	is a typed `sorry`. -/
396	instance of_regular (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R]
397	    [IsNoetherianRing R] [IsRegularLocalRing R] : CohenMacaulay R where
398	  depth_eq_krullDim := by
399	    sorry
400	
401	end CohenMacaulay
402	
403	end RingTheory

1	\chapter{Auslander--Buchsbaum}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.b sub-row of Route A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the corollary ``regular local ring
8	% $\Rightarrow$ Cohen--Macaulay'' that A.4.a (the codim-1 extension proof for
9	% Albanese morphisms out of a regular projective surface) consumes. Per
10	% STRATEGY.md L30 this row is gated downstream on A.4.a but is independently
11	% startable on the Mathlib side: the algebra here is decoupled from the
12	% Albanese geometry and lives entirely under \texttt{RingTheory.*}. The
13	% target Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean}
14	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
15	% creating it. Note that Mathlib already houses parts of the depth /
16	% projective-dimension API
17	% (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Depth.*},
18	% \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.RegularSequence},
19	% \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.ProjectiveResolution}); a Plan-Agent audit
20	% at the pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}) should confirm what is present
21	% before the prover writes the file, since a fully Mathlib-backed
22	% Auslander--Buchsbaum would collapse this chapter to a re-export.
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface $S$ over a field --- such as $\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1$
28	or the product $C \times C$ of a smooth projective curve $C$ with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension $2$. To extend a rational map
30	$f : S \dashrightarrow A$ from $S$ to an abelian variety across a codimension-$2$ closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring $\mathcal{O}_{S,x}$ at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} $2$, hence a rational function with no codimension-$1$ poles on a neighbourhood of $x$
33	extends across $x$. The depth $\geq 2$ inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring $R$ one has $\text{pd}_R(R) = 0$,
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension $d$ admits an $R$-regular sequence of length $d$ generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains $\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)$, i.e.\ $R$ is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its $\text{Ext}$ reformulation
47	    (Definition~\ref{def:depth} and Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_via_ext}),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~\ref{def:projective_dimension}),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~\ref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}), and
54	  \item the corollary regular $\Rightarrow$ Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~\ref{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let $R$ be a ring, and $I \subset R$ an ideal. Let $M$ be a finite $R$-module.
74	  %  The {\it $I$-depth} of $M$, denoted $\text{depth}_I(M)$, is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if $IM \not = M$, then $\text{depth}_I(M)$ is the supremum in
77	  %  $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}$ of the lengths of $M$-regular sequences in $I$,
78	  %  \item if $IM = M$ we set $\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty$.
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If $(R, \mathfrak m)$ is local we call $\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)$ simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of $M$."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let $R$ be a commutative ring, let $I \subseteq R$ be an ideal, and let $M$ be a finitely
84	  generated $R$-module. The \emph{$I$-depth} of $M$, written $\text{depth}_I(M)$, is the
85	  supremum in $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}$ of the lengths of $M$-regular sequences contained
86	  in $I$, provided $IM \neq M$; if $IM = M$ we set $\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty$. When
87	  $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  $\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)$ and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of $M$. The depth of the ring $R$ itself is $\text{depth}(R)$, the depth
90	  of $R$ as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence $f_1, \dots, f_r \in R$ is called \emph{$M$-regular} if
94	$M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0$ and for each $i$, the element $f_i$ is a non-zero-divisor on
95	$M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M$. The empty sequence is regular on $M$ iff $M \neq 0$ (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention $\text{depth}(0) = \infty$
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the $\text{Ext}$ characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via $\text{Ext}$]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  \uses{def:depth}
110	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
111	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % "Let $R$ be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal $\mathfrak m$.
114	  %  Let $M$ be a nonzero finite $R$-module. Then $\text{depth}(M)$
115	  %  is equal to the smallest integer $i$ such that
116	  %  $\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)$ is nonzero."
117	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
118	  Let $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
119	  $\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}$ and let $M$ be a nonzero finitely generated $R$-module. Then
120	  \[
121	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
122	  \]
123	\end{lemma}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
126	% "Let $\delta(M)$ denote the depth of $M$ and let $i(M)$ denote
127	%  the smallest integer $i$ such that $\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)$
128	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that $i(M) < \infty$.
129	%  By Lemma \ref{lemma-ideal-nonzerodivisor} we have
130	%  $\delta(M) = 0$ if and only if $i(M) = 0$, because
131	%  $\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)$ exactly means
132	%  that $i(M) = 0$. Hence if $\delta(M)$ or $i(M)$ is $> 0$, then we may
133	%  choose $x \in \mathfrak m$ such that (a) $x$ is a nonzerodivisor
134	%  on $M$, and (b) $\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1$.
135	%  Consider the long exact sequence
136	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
137	%  $0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0$ by Lemma \ref{lemma-long-exact-seq-ext}:
138	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
139	%  Since $x \in \mathfrak m$ all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
140	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
141	%  Lemma \ref{lemma-annihilate-ext}.
142	%  Thus it is clear that $i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1$. Induction on
143	%  $\delta(M)$ finishes the proof."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  \uses{def:depth}
146	  Write $\delta := \text{depth}(M)$ and $i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}$.
147	  The base case $\delta = 0$ is exactly $\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0$ (some element of
148	  $M$ is annihilated by $\mathfrak{m}$, i.e.\ $\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)$, i.e.\
149	  $\mathfrak{m}$ contains a zero-divisor on $M$), which is $i = 0$. For the inductive step
150	  pick $x \in \mathfrak{m}$ a non-zero-divisor on $M$ with
151	  $\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1$. The short exact sequence
152	  $0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0$ induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
153	  -)$. Multiplication by $x$ annihilates each $\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
154	  $x \in \mathfrak{m}$ and $\kappa$ is annihilated by $\mathfrak{m}$), so the long exact
155	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
156	  $\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ at the relevant
157	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by $1$. By induction
158	  $i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1$, so $i = \delta$.
159	\end{proof}
160	
161	\section{Projective dimension}
162	\label{sec:ab_pd}
163	
164	\begin{definition}
165	
166	\leanok
167	  [Projective dimension]
168	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
169	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
170	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
171	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
172	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
173	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
174	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
175	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
176	  Let $R$ be a commutative ring and let $M$ be an $R$-module. The \emph{projective
177	  dimension} of $M$, written $\text{pd}_R(M)$, is the infimum in
178	  $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}$ of the lengths $n$ of projective resolutions
179	  \[
180	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
181	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
182	  \]
183	  with each $P_i$ a projective $R$-module. We say $M$ has \emph{finite projective dimension}
184	  if $\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty$, and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
185	  convention $\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty$ (some references); we use $\text{pd}_R(0) = 0$ here
186	  (the empty resolution $0 \to 0$ of length $0$), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
187	  Theorem~\ref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} concerns only nonzero $M$.
188	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated $R$-module of finite projective
189	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each $P_i$
190	  free of finite rank and each transition map $P_{i+1} \to P_i$ landing in $\mathfrak{m} P_i$.
191	\end{definition}
192	
193	The two basic facts about $\text{pd}_R(M)$ needed downstream are:
194	\begin{itemize}
195	  \item $\text{pd}_R(M) = 0$ iff $M$ is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
196	    iff $M$ is free);
197	  \item if $0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0$ is a short exact sequence with $P$ projective, then
198	    $\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1$ provided $K \neq 0$.
199	\end{itemize}
200	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
201	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.
202	
203	\section{Depth on a short exact sequence}
204	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}
205	
206	\begin{lemma}
207	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
208	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
209	  \lean{RingTheory.depth_of_short_exact}
210	  \uses{def:depth, lem:depth_via_ext}
211	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
212	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
213	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
214	  % "Let $R$ be a local Noetherian ring. Let $0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0$
215	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite $R$-modules.
216	  %  \begin{enumerate}
217	  %  \item
218	  %  $\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}$
219	  %  \item
220	  %  $\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}$
221	  %  \item
222	  %  $\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}$
223	  %  \end{enumerate}"
224	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
225	  Let $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ be a Noetherian local ring and let
226	  $0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0$ be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
227	  $R$-modules. Then
228	  \begin{enumerate}
229	    \item $\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)$;
230	    \item $\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)$;
231	    \item $\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)$.
232	  \end{enumerate}
233	\end{lemma}
234	
235	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
236	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
237	%  $\Ext^i(\kappa, N)$, see Lemma \ref{lemma-depth-ext},
238	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
239	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
240	%  from Lemma \ref{lemma-long-exact-seq-ext}."
241	\begin{proof}
242	  \uses{lem:depth_via_ext}
243	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_via_ext}, $\text{depth}(N')$ (resp.\ $\text{depth}(N)$,
244	  $\text{depth}(N'')$) is the smallest index at which $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)$ is
245	  nonzero on $N'$ (resp.\ $N$, $N''$). Apply the long exact $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)$
246	  sequence to $0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0$:
247	  \[
248	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
249	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
250	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
251	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
252	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
253	  \]
254	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
255	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
256	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
257	  the proof of Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_via_ext}.
258	\end{proof}
259	
260	\section{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula}
261	\label{sec:ab_main}
262	
263	\begin{theorem}
264	
265	\leanok
266	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum formula]
267	  \label{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
268	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula}
269	  \uses{def:depth, def:projective_dimension, lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
270	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum)
271	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L27207-L27280).
272	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
273	  % "Let $R$ be a Noetherian local ring. Let $M$ be a nonzero finite $R$-module
274	  %  which has finite projective dimension $\text{pd}_R(M)$. Then we have
275	  %  $$
276	  %  \text{depth}(R) = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)
277	  %  $$"
278	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum); cf.\
279	  Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Theorem 19.1; Auslander--Buchsbaum,
280	  \emph{Homological dimension in local rings}, 1957.}
281	  Let $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ be a Noetherian local ring and let $M$ be a nonzero finitely
282	  generated $R$-module of finite projective dimension. Then
283	  \[
284	    \text{pd}_R(M) \;+\; \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \text{depth}(R).
285	  \]
286	\end{theorem}
287	
288	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
289	% "We prove this by induction on $\text{depth}(M)$. The most interesting
290	%  case is the case $\text{depth}(M) = 0$. In this case, let
291	%  $$

293	%  $$
294	%  be a minimal finite free resolution, so $e = \text{pd}_R(M)$.
295	%  By Lemma \ref{lemma-add-trivial-complex} we may assume all matrix

297	%  ideal of $R$. Then on the one hand, by
298	%  Proposition \ref{proposition-what-exact} we see that
299	%  $\text{depth}(R) \geq e$. On the other hand, breaking the long
300	%  exact sequence into short exact sequences
301	%  \begin{align*}
302	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e - 1}} \to K_{e - 2} \to 0,\\
303	%  0 \to K_{e - 2} \to R^{n_{e - 2}} \to K_{e - 3} \to 0,\\
304	%  \ldots,\\
305	%  0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
306	%  \end{align*}
307	%  we see, using Lemma \ref{lemma-depth-in-ses}, that
308	%  \begin{align*}
309	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1,\\
310	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2,\\
311	%  \ldots,\\
312	%  \text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e - 1),\\
313	%  \text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e
314	%  \end{align*}
315	%  and since $\text{depth}(M) = 0$ we conclude $\text{depth}(R) \leq e$.
316	%  This finishes the proof of the case $\text{depth}(M) = 0$.
317	%
318	%  Induction step. If $\text{depth}(M) > 0$, then we pick $x \in \mathfrak m$
319	%  which is a nonzerodivisor on both $M$ and $R$. This is possible, because
320	%  either $\text{pd}_R(M) > 0$ and $\text{depth}(R) > 0$ by the aforementioned
321	%  Proposition \ref{proposition-what-exact} or $\text{pd}_R(M) = 0$ in which
322	%  case $M$ is finite free hence also $\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0$.
323	%  Thus $\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0$ by Lemma \ref{lemma-depth-in-ses}
324	%  (for example) and we can find an $x \in \mathfrak m$ which is a nonzerodivisor
325	%  on both $R$ and $M$. Let
326	%  $$
327	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0

329	%  be a minimal resolution as above. An application of the snake lemma
330	%  shows that
331	%  $$
332	%  0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to
333	%  M/xM \to 0
334	%  $$
335	%  is a minimal resolution too. Thus $\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM)$.
336	%  By Lemma \ref{lemma-depth-drops-by-one} we have
337	%  $\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1$ and
338	%  $\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1$.
339	%  Till now depths have all been depths as $R$ modules, but we observe that
340	%  $\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)$ and similarly for $R/xR$.
341	%  By induction hypothesis we see that the
342	%  Auslander-Buchsbaum formula holds for $M/xM$ over $R/xR$. Since the
343	%  depths of both $R/xR$ and $M/xM$ have decreased by one and the projective
344	%  dimension has not changed we conclude."
345	\begin{proof}
346	  \uses{def:depth, def:projective_dimension, lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
347	  We induct on $\text{depth}(M)$.
348	
349	  \emph{Base case $\text{depth}(M) = 0$.} Let $e := \text{pd}_R(M)$ and pick a minimal
350	  finite free resolution
351	  \[
352	    0 \longrightarrow R^{n_e} \longrightarrow R^{n_{e-1}} \longrightarrow
353	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
354	  \]
355	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in $\mathfrak{m}$ (this is the
356	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
357	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
358	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
359	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of $r$-minors) gives
360	  $\text{depth}(R) \geq e$. On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
361	  sequences
362	  \[
363	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
364	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
365	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
366	  \]
367	  and applying Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence} iteratively,
368	  yields the chain of inequalities
369	  $\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1$,
370	  $\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2$, \dots,
371	  $\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)$, and finally
372	  $\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e$. Since $\text{depth}(M) = 0$ we deduce
373	  $\text{depth}(R) \leq e$. Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
374	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
375	
376	  \emph{Inductive step $\text{depth}(M) > 0$.} We claim there exists $x \in \mathfrak{m}$
377	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both $R$ and $M$. Indeed: either $\text{pd}_R(M) > 0$, in
378	  which case $\text{depth}(R) > 0$ by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
379	  of a minimal resolution, or $\text{pd}_R(M) = 0$ in which case $M$ is finite free and
380	  $\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0$. Either way both $\text{depth}(R) > 0$ and
381	  $\text{depth}(M) > 0$, hence $\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0$ by
382	  Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}, so a common non-zero-divisor $x \in
383	  \mathfrak{m}$ exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of $M$ as above; the snake
384	  lemma applied to multiplication by $x$ gives a minimal finite free resolution
385	  \[
386	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
387	  \]
388	  of $M/xM$ over $R/xR$, of the same length $e$. So
389	  $\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)$. By Stacks
390	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both $\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1$ and
391	  $\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1$, and one verifies
392	  $\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)$ (regular sequences in $\mathfrak{m}$
393	  correspond to regular sequences in $\mathfrak{m}/(x)$). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
394	  $M/xM$ over $R/xR$:
395	  \[
396	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
397	  \]
398	  Substituting yields
399	  $\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1$, i.e.\
400	  $\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)$, as required.
401	\end{proof}
402	
403	\section{Corollary: regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay}
404	\label{sec:ab_regular_cm}
405	
406	\begin{definition}
407	
408	\leanok
409	  [Cohen--Macaulay local ring]
410	  \label{def:cohen_macaulay_local}
411	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay}
412	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM)
413	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25288-L25299).
414	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
415	  % "A Noetherian local ring $R$ is called {\it Cohen-Macaulay}
416	  %  if it is Cohen-Macaulay as a module over itself."
417	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM).}
418	  A Noetherian local ring $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ is called \emph{Cohen--Macaulay} if
419	  $\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)$ (equivalently, if $R$ is Cohen--Macaulay as a module
420	  over itself, where Cohen--Macaulay for a finite $R$-module $M$ means
421	  $\dim(\text{Supp}(M)) = \text{depth}(M)$, cf.\ Stacks tag 00N3).
422	\end{definition}
423	
424	\begin{corollary}
425	
426	\leanok
427	  [Regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay]
428	  \label{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
429	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular}
430	  \uses{def:depth, def:cohen_macaulay_local, thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
431	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM)
432	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25714-L25730).
433	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
434	  % "Let $R$ be a regular local ring and let
435	  %  $x_1, \ldots, x_d$ be a minimal set of generators
436	  %  for the maximal ideal $\mathfrak m$. Then
437	  %  $x_1, \ldots, x_d$ is a regular sequence, and
438	  %  each $R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c)$ is a regular local ring
439	  %  of dimension $d - c$. In particular $R$ is Cohen-Macaulay."
440	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM).}
441	  Every regular Noetherian local ring is Cohen--Macaulay: if $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ is
442	  regular local of Krull dimension $d$, then $\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)$.
443	\end{corollary}
444	
445	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
446	% "Note that $R/x_1R$ is a Noetherian local ring of dimension $\geq d - 1$
447	%  by Lemma \ref{lemma-one-equation} with $x_2, \ldots, x_d$
448	%  generating the maximal ideal. Hence it is a regular local ring by definition.
449	%  Since $R$ is a domain by Lemma \ref{lemma-regular-domain}
450	%  $x_1$ is a nonzerodivisor."
451	\begin{proof}
452	  \uses{def:depth, def:cohen_macaulay_local}
453	  Let $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ be regular of dimension $d$ and pick a minimal generating set
454	  $x_1, \dots, x_d$ of $\mathfrak{m}$. By Stacks tag 00NQ (lemma-regular-domain), $R$ is
455	  an integral domain, so $x_1$ is a non-zero-divisor. The quotient $R/x_1 R$ is a Noetherian
456	  local ring of dimension $\geq d - 1$ (by Krull's principal ideal theorem,
457	  \texttt{lemma-one-equation}) with maximal ideal generated by $x_2, \dots, x_d$, hence
458	  has dimension exactly $d - 1$ and is again regular of dimension $d - 1$. Inducting on $d$,
459	  $x_1, \dots, x_d$ is an $R$-regular sequence, so
460	  $\text{depth}(R) \geq d$. The reverse inequality $\text{depth}(R) \leq \dim(R) = d$
461	  always holds for any nonzero finite module over a Noetherian local ring
462	  (Stacks tag 00LK, \texttt{lemma-bound-depth}). Thus $\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)$, i.e.\
463	  $R$ is Cohen--Macaulay.
464	
465	  \emph{Remark on the Auslander--Buchsbaum route.} The same conclusion can be obtained from
466	  Theorem~\ref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} as follows: take $M := R$ itself. Then $M$ is
467	  finitely generated and projective (free of rank $1$), so $\text{pd}_R(R) = 0$. The
468	  formula gives $\text{depth}(R) = 0 + \text{depth}(R)$, a tautology. The non-tautological
469	  use of Auslander--Buchsbaum is rather: for a regular local ring of dimension $d$, every
470	  finitely generated module $M$ has $\text{pd}_R(M) \leq d$ (Stacks tag 00O2,
471	  \texttt{proposition-finite-gl-dim-regular}); applying the formula to such an $M$ then
472	  relates $\text{depth}(M)$ to $d - \text{pd}_R(M)$, which is the form most often used in
473	  practice. The direct regular-sequence argument above is the cleanest proof of
474	  $R$ Cohen--Macaulay itself.
475	\end{proof}
476	
477	\section{Application: codim-1 extension on a regular surface (gating A.4.a)}
478	\label{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}
479	
480	The downstream consumer is A.4.a: extending a rational map
481	$f : S \dashrightarrow A$ from a regular projective surface $S$ to an abelian variety $A$
482	across a closed point $x \in S$ that is missing from the domain of definition $U \subseteq S$.
483	The classical mechanism (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.7) is:
484	
485	\begin{enumerate}
486	  \item The local ring $\mathcal{O}_{S,x}$ is a regular Noetherian local ring of Krull
487	    dimension $2$ (because $S$ is regular and $\dim S = 2$).
488	  \item By Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} it is Cohen--Macaulay, so
489	    $\text{depth}(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}) = 2$. Equivalently, the maximal ideal contains an
490	    $\mathcal{O}_{S,x}$-regular sequence of length $2$ (any two transverse generators).
491	  \item Sections of the structure sheaf $\mathcal{O}_S$ on $U = S \setminus \{x\}$ extend
492	    uniquely across $x$ because $H^0_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0$ and
493	    $H^1_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0$ (these local-cohomology vanishings are equivalent to depth
494	    $\geq 2$ at $x$ by Stacks tag 0AVF; see Hartshorne, III.3.5, for the surface case).
495	  \item For a rational map $f : S \dashrightarrow A$ with codimension-$\geq 2$ indeterminacy
496	    locus on a regular surface, the same depth-$\geq 2$ input produces a sheaf-theoretic
497	    extension of $f$ to all of $S$: pull back any sheaf $\mathcal{O}_A$-affine-locally,
498	    obtain sections over $U$, and extend across $x$ by the above.
499	\end{enumerate}
500	
501	So Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} is the gating input: the geometric A.4.a
502	proof can be written conditional on the algebraic Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary, and the
503	two halves develop independently --- as long as Theorem~\ref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and
504	Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} are formalized in
505	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} with the signatures pinned
506	in this chapter, A.4.a can consume them as Mathlib-style imports.
507	
508	\section{Lean encoding}
509	\label{sec:ab_lean_encoding}
510	
511	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean}.
512	Mathlib at the project's pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}) already exposes substantial parts
513	of the depth / projective-dimension API:
514	\begin{itemize}
515	  \item \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.RegularSequence} provides
516	    \texttt{RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular} (definition of $M$-regular sequences),
517	    matching Definition~\ref{def:depth}.
518	  \item \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Depth} (if present at the pinned commit) provides
519	    \texttt{Module.depth} as the supremum of regular-sequence lengths; the prover
520	    should re-export this as \texttt{RingTheory.Module.depth} rather than re-define.
521	  \item \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension} (or
522	    \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution}) provides
523	    \texttt{Module.projectiveDimension} via finite projective resolutions, matching
524	    Definition~\ref{def:projective_dimension}.
525	  \item \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.CohenMacaulay} (in some recent revisions) provides
526	    \texttt{IsCohenMacaulayLocalRing}, matching Definition~\ref{def:cohen_macaulay_local}.
527	\end{itemize}
528	The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula itself (Theorem~\ref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}) is the
529	content the project must supply unless Mathlib has it at the pinned commit (the Plan Agent
530	should confirm via \texttt{lean\_local\_search} on \texttt{auslander\_buchsbaum} /
531	\texttt{Module.auslanderBuchsbaum} / similar). If absent, the file will:
532	\begin{enumerate}
533	  \item State Theorem~\ref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} as the formula
534	    $\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}_R(M) = \text{depth}(R)$ under the hypotheses
535	    ``$(R, \mathfrak{m})$ Noetherian local, $M$ finitely generated, $\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty$,
536	    $M \neq 0$''.
537	  \item Prove it by induction on $\text{depth}(M)$ following the structure of the
538	    proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} above, importing the supporting
539	    Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence} from Mathlib's depth API or
540	    proving it in tandem.
541	  \item Export Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} as
542	    \texttt{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of\_regular} (or whatever name Mathlib uses if
543	    already present) as the consumer-facing statement.
544	\end{enumerate}
545	No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a homological-algebra construction.
546	The chapter is independent of the rest of Route A's geometric infrastructure (cube of
547	abelian varieties, theorem-of-the-square, Albanese universal property), making it a good
548	parallel-work candidate.
549	
550	\section{Out of scope}
551	\label{sec:ab_out_of_scope}
552	
553	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the regular
554	$\Rightarrow$ Cohen--Macaulay corollary as required by A.4.a. The following downstream
555	constructions are explicitly out of scope here:
556	\begin{itemize}
557	  \item \textbf{The A.4.a codim-1-to-codim-2 extension proof itself} --- consumes
558	    Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}, but the geometric extension argument
559	    lives in its sibling chapter \texttt{Albanese\_CodimExtension.tex} (planned).
560	  \item \textbf{Local-cohomology vanishing $H^i_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0$ for $i < \text{depth}$}
561	    --- a downstream consequence of Stacks tag 0AVF; planned for its own chapter
562	    \texttt{Albanese\_LocalCohomology.tex} or as part of A.4.a directly.
563	  \item \textbf{Global Cohen--Macaulay schemes / global depth} --- this chapter only treats
564	    the local-ring case. The global-to-local reduction is standard but not packaged here.
565	  \item \textbf{General homological-dimension theory (global dimension, finitistic
566	    dimension)} --- only the local-ring projective-dimension invariant appears here.
567	  \item \textbf{Cohen--Macaulay characterisations via $\omega_R$, dualising complex,
568	    Gorenstein refinement} --- the formula and its immediate Cohen--Macaulay corollary
569	    suffice for A.4.a; the duality side is not needed.
570	\end{itemize}

module

prelude
public import Lean.Meta.Reduce
public import Lean.Elab.Eval
public import Lean.Elab.Command
public import Lean.Elab.Open

import Init.Data.Order.Lemmas
